titleStmt.title: Observation of ~100% valley-coherent excitons in monolayer MoS 2 through giant enhancement of valley coherence time

analytic.title: Observation of ~100% valley-coherent excitons in monolayer MoS 2 through giant enhancement of valley coherence time

application.desc: GROBID - A machine learning software for extracting information from scholarly documents

div.p: A demonstration of the excitons remaining valley coherent during the entire radiative recombination time in few-layer graphene capped monolayer MoS2.

div.head: 𝑑𝑑𝐒𝐒(𝐐𝐐) 𝑑𝑑𝑡𝑡
div.formula: = 𝛀𝛀(𝐐𝐐) × 𝐒𝐒(𝐐𝐐) + � W 𝐐𝐐𝐐𝐐 ′ [𝐒𝐒(𝐐𝐐 ′ ) -𝐒𝐒(𝐐𝐐)] 𝐐𝐐 ′ - 1 𝜏𝜏 𝐒𝐒(𝐐𝐐) + 𝐆𝐆 (𝟏𝟏)
div.p: Here W 𝐐𝐐𝐐𝐐 ′ is the rate of any generic momentum scattering mechanism, e.g., exciton-impurity and exciton-phonon scattering. 𝜏𝜏 is the net exciton lifetime given by 1 𝜏𝜏 � = 1 𝜏𝜏 𝑟𝑟 � + 1 𝜏𝜏 𝑛𝑛𝑟𝑟 � + 1 𝜏𝜏 𝑓𝑓𝑓𝑓𝑓𝑓𝑓𝑓𝑓𝑓𝑟𝑟 � , where 𝜏𝜏 𝑟𝑟 , 𝜏𝜏 𝑛𝑛𝑟𝑟 , and 𝜏𝜏 𝑓𝑓𝑓𝑓𝑓𝑓𝑓𝑓𝑓𝑓𝑟𝑟 are the radiative, non-radiative, and filtering timescale.

Filtering is a non-radiative process where the excitons are scattered out of the light cone, e.g., scattering to lower energy states, interlayer transfer to graphene, etc., in which case, the light collection is limited to 𝑡𝑡 ≤ 𝜏𝜏 𝑓𝑓𝑓𝑓𝑓𝑓𝑓𝑓𝑓𝑓𝑟𝑟 . 𝐆𝐆 represents the exciton generation rate. On recombination, the DOLP of this mixed state is given by 〈𝑆𝑆 𝑥𝑥 〉, averaged over 𝐐𝐐 values within the light cone (see Supplementary Note 2 for proof).

The possible ways to improve the valley coherence time are: (a) by minimizing scattering (W 𝐐𝐐𝐐𝐐 ′ ) inside the light cone such that 𝐒𝐒 does not accumulate random phase by precessing around 𝛀𝛀(𝐐𝐐), or by enhancing W 𝐐𝐐𝐐𝐐 ′ such that the whole operation is pushed towards the motional narrowing regime (simulation results in Figure 1c); (b) by screening the electron-hole interaction which results in reduced exchange interaction, and in turn a suppressed 𝛀𝛀 (middle panel of Figure 1d). However, a side-effect of the enhanced screening is an increment in the exciton lifetime due to a reduction in the binding energy. This can be overcome by (c) reducing
𝜏𝜏 by introducing a fast-filtering mechanism [21][22][23] (bottom panel of Figure 1d).

To understand the interplay among these factors systematically, we prepare four different stacks of monolayer MoS2 combined with hBN and FLG, which are: (1) hBN-MoS2-hBN (HMH), (2) FLG-hBN-MoS2-hBN-FLG (GHMHG), (3) MoS2-FLG-hBN (MGH) and ( 4)

FLG-MoS2-FLG (GMG) (see Methods). We obtain an exciton DOLP of 44.5 (±10)% in the HMH stack, 37 (±9)% in the GHMHG stack, 77 (±5)% in the MGH stack, and 96 (±6)% in the GMG stack on 633 nm near resonant laser excitation at 5 K. Linear polarization-resolved representative PL spectra in Figure 2a-d and the bar diagram (with error bars) in Figure 2e compare the DOLP numbers in all the four stacks (more spectra in Supplementary Figure 3456).

Interestingly, there are several spots where we observe ~100% DOLP in the GMG stack (Figure 2d and Supplementary Figure 6). In Supplementary Figure 7a, we show similar results of ~100% DOLP obtained from few-layer graphene encapsulated monolayer WS2 (GWG) stack. We also perform polarization dependent time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) measurement and obtain a peak DOLP of 97.6% in the GWG stack (see Supplementary Figure 7b).

We would like to highlight some additional observations on the GMG stack before the main analysis begins: (1) As a result of FLG encapsulation, the PL spectra predominantly consist of the clean 𝐴𝐴 1𝑠𝑠 exciton peak 22 . The spectra of FLG encapsulated monolayer MoS2, MoSe2, and WS2 on 532 nm excitation are shown in Figure 2f, clearly indicating suppression of spurious peaks from defect-bound excitons and other excitonic complexes. We also observe a clear 𝐴𝐴 2𝑠𝑠 peak located at 44 (32) meV higher than the 𝐴𝐴 1𝑠𝑠 peak in MoS2 (MoSe2 and WS2) due to enhanced screening 24 .

(2) We also get a very high degree of circular polarization (DOCP) of 81.6 (±2) % in the GMG stack, much larger compared to the 20.5 (±9) % DOCP in the HMH stack (Figure 2g-h and more spectra in Supplementary Figure 89). The in-plane nature of 𝛀𝛀 explains this observation that DOCP is smaller than DOLP for 2D excitons, which is consistent with previous reports 10,25 (see Supplementary Note 3). This indicates that starting with a linear polarization (that is, on the equator of the Bloch sphere) is the most favourable scenario to maintain valley coherence compared with any other (elliptical) polarization. (3) The initial and the final state in the Raman scattering process coincides with the 𝐴𝐴 2𝑠𝑠 and the 𝐴𝐴 1𝑠𝑠 exciton level, respectively, on 633 nm excitation at 5 K in the GMG stack. This dual resonance enhances the intensity of the Raman peaks significantly (represented by the dashed lines in Figure 2d and 2h) and enables the observation of other less commonly observed modes distinctly (Supplementary Figure 10). The fact that the excitation laser is resonant with the 2s state in the GMG stack and we observe almost fully-coherent 1s excitonic emission from the stack, it is likely that the generated 2s excitons relax through polarization preserving processes, such as, dipole-coupled radiative transition (2s→2p→1s).

In order to establish the different degrees of screening in the stacks, we plot the PL spectra for the HMH, GHMHG, and the GMG stack obtained from 532 nm excitation in Figure 3a-c. The 𝐴𝐴 2𝑠𝑠 -𝐴𝐴 1𝑠𝑠 energy separation obtained is 144.5 meV in the HMH stack, which reduces to 60 and 45 meV in the GHMHG and GMG stack, respectively. To get an estimate of the 𝐴𝐴 1𝑠𝑠 exciton binding energy change (Figure 3d), we obtain the continuum of the exciton energy spectrum by numerically solving the Bethe-Salpeter equation 26 using a two-band Hamiltonian.

In the calculation, the parameters are fitted such that the experimentally obtained 𝐴𝐴 2𝑠𝑠 -𝐴𝐴 1𝑠𝑠 energy separation matches with the calculated one (Supplementary Note 4 and Supplementary Figure 2). The calculated 𝐴𝐴 1𝑠𝑠 exciton binding energy is 379 meV in the HMH system, which reduces to 162.5 and 122 meV in the GHMHG and the GMG stacks respectively due to graphene induced screening.

One immediate consequence of such a screening is the reduction in the inter-valley exchange interaction 25 . The exchange interaction is composed of two components -the short-range part and the long-range part. The short-range component is zero at 𝐐𝐐 = 𝟎𝟎 due to the three-fold rotational symmetry condition and is negligible at higher 𝐐𝐐 values. The long-range part is given by 27 :
div.formula: 𝐽𝐽 𝐐𝐐 𝐿𝐿𝐿𝐿 ∝ - |∑ 𝜓𝜓(𝐤𝐤) 𝐤𝐤 | 2 𝐸𝐸 𝑔𝑔 2 𝑉𝑉(𝐐𝐐)|𝐐𝐐| 2
Here |∑ 𝜓𝜓(𝐤𝐤) 𝐤𝐤 | 2 𝐴𝐴 = |𝜓𝜓(𝑟𝑟 𝑓𝑓ℎ = 0)| 2
div.p: is the electron-hole wavefunction overlap at zero relative separation (𝑟𝑟 𝑓𝑓ℎ = 0), 𝐤𝐤 is the reciprocal space wave-vector, 𝐸𝐸 𝑔𝑔 denotes the bandgap of MoS2, and 𝑉𝑉(𝐐𝐐) is the electron-hole coulomb interaction. The dielectric screening modulates the following factors: (a) |𝜓𝜓(𝑟𝑟 𝑓𝑓ℎ = 0)| 2due to a reduction in the 2D exciton binding energy 24,28 ;

(b) 𝐸𝐸 𝑔𝑔due to bandgap renormalization effect in monolayer TMDs 29,30 ; and (c) 𝑉𝑉(𝐐𝐐)due to suppressed electron-hole interaction 31 . In Figure 3e, we show the variation in 𝐽𝐽 𝐐𝐐 𝐿𝐿𝐿𝐿 with 𝐐𝐐 within the light cone, and hence the screening induced suppression of the long-range exchange in our samples (see Supplementary Note 4).

Another consequence of screening is the enhancement of the exciton radiative lifetime due to a reduced electron-hole wavefunction overlap. Here, a longer lifetime is undesirable as it leads to a larger valley decoherence (Figure 1a). To estimate the exciton lifetime and its role in the valley decoherence, we carry out TRPL measurements on our samples (see Methods and Supplementary Figure 11). The TRPL values obtained in our stacks are as follows: < 5 ps in the HMH stack, 6 -8 ps in the GHMHG stack, and < 5 ps in the GMG stack. The uncertainty in the lifetime in the HMH and GMG stacks arises as it is smaller than the 10% limit of our Instrument Response Function (IRF) width 32 . Nonetheless, several reports supporting these numbers are already available in literature 18,19,22,33 . Moreover, the qualitative trend in the exciton lifetime testifies the anticipated trend in Figure 1d, showing a clear enhancement in the exciton lifetime in the GHMHG stack, as compared to the other two stacks.

Obtaining an average DOLP of only 44.5 (±10) % implies an ultra-short valley coherence time in the HMH stack, in agreement with previous reports 12,15 . On the other hand, in the GHMHG stack, an increased exciton lifetime is an evidence of screening induced enhancement of exciton lifetime as a result of introducing top and bottom FLG. Due to the opposing roles of reduced exchange and increased lifetime, we do not observe any improvement in the exciton DOLP in this sample compared with the HMH sample.

This side-effect of screening driven enhanced exciton lifetime is eliminated in the GMG stack through filtering, where light collection from the long-lived excitons is prohibited due to a fast transfer of excitons to graphene. The extracted timescale corresponds to the graphene-transferlimited exciton lifetime in this system. This timescale is similar to that in the HMH stack, and in agreement with previous report 22,33 . Therefore, the significant exciton DOLP difference between the HMH and the GMG stacks is thus attributed to screening modified exchange interaction without any confounding effect due to a change in the exciton lifetime.
To obtain a quantitative understanding, we solve the steady-state form of the MSS equation:
div.formula: 𝐆𝐆 = 1 𝜏𝜏 𝐒𝐒(𝐐𝐐) -𝛀𝛀(𝐐𝐐) × 𝐒𝐒(𝐐𝐐) -� 𝑤𝑤 𝑄𝑄 2 sin 2 𝛼𝛼 2 [𝐒𝐒(𝐐𝐐 ′ ) -𝐒𝐒(𝐐𝐐)] 𝐐𝐐 ′(3)
div.p: We obtain the DOLP (〈𝑆𝑆 𝑥𝑥 〉) for the 𝐴𝐴 1𝑠𝑠 exciton in the HMH and the GMG stack (Supplementary Note 4). For W 𝐐𝐐𝐐𝐐 ′ , the exciton-impurity scattering rate expression is used (Supplementary Note 4.2). 𝜏𝜏 is the exciton lifetime, 𝛼𝛼 is the angle between the initial (𝐐𝐐) and the final (𝐐𝐐 ′ ) state and 𝑤𝑤 is an overall scaling factor. We neglect the contribution of excitonphonon scattering in decoherence at 5 K. Plotted in Figure 4 is the variation in 〈𝑆𝑆 𝑥𝑥 〉 as a function of the 𝑤𝑤 for the two stacks. In both the cases, the V shaped variation is understood as follows:

For small 𝑤𝑤, an increase in the scattering degrades the valley coherence due to enhanced exciton precession around 𝛀𝛀 (Figure 1a). However, this effect is non-monotonic, as on significantly enhancing the scattering rate, the DOLP starts increasing after reaching a minimum. This phenomenon is referred to as motional narrowing 10,34 , and arises due to a cancellation of the accumulated randomness in the phase information of 𝐒𝐒. Mathematically, the system is in the motional narrowing regime when the exciton scattering frequency becomes larger than the precession frequency, leading to a longer pseudospin coherence time.
We take the extracted homogeneous (Lorentzian component of the Voigt fitting) linewidth (Γ ℎ𝑜𝑜𝑜𝑜 ) of the co-polarized exciton PL peak as the experimental analogue of the scattering rate.

The experimental DOLP as a function of the Lorentzian linewidth, superimposed on the simulation results, is shown in Figure 4. Both in GMG and HMH stacks, the extracted value of Γ ℎ𝑜𝑜𝑜𝑜 is much larger than the exciton lifetime limited linewidth, as obtained from TRPL. This suggests that the impurity scattering rate is similar in both the samples, and it dominates over other linewidth broadening mechanisms. This is also evident from the upward trend of the experimental DOLP with Γ ℎ𝑜𝑜𝑜𝑜 , which is in excellent agreement with the rising side of 〈𝑆𝑆 𝑥𝑥 〉 versus 𝑤𝑤 in the simulation. This suggests that the whole operation lies in the motional narrowing regime in both the samples.

We conclude that the combined effect of enhanced screening, reduced lifetime due to interlayer transfer, and motional narrowing helps us to achieve ~100% exciton DOLP in our FLGcapped MoS2 sample. This is a direct evidence of valley coherence being maintained during the entire exciton lifetime by cutting down the decoherence channels before the spontaneous emission. Achieving ~100% steady-state DOLP also indicates that the true valley coherence timescale beyond few picoseconds in the GMG stack, which is in excellent agreement with simulation result obtained by solving time-dependent MSS equation (see Supplementary Figure 12).

The combination of such ~100% polarization, coupled with background-free, narrow linewidth emission, makes the GMG stack a promising substrate for spectral diffusion-free, indistinguishable single photon source. As the initialized coherence in the exciton is shown to be staying protected for a longer time, the results have intriguing prospects on performing experiments involving coherent manipulation of exciton and building quantum system operating at these timescales.

div.head: Methods
div.p: Sample preparation: All the stacks in this paper are prepared first by mechanically exfoliating the layered material on a Polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) film, and then its subsequent transfer in a controlled manner underneath a microscope on a Si substrate covered with 285 nm thick thermally grown SiO2. The thickness of the few-layer graphene is chosen to be 2-3 nm in all the stacks. The thickness of hBN layers is in the range of 20-30 nm apart from the GHMHG stack. To ensure strong screening in the GHMHG stack, the hBN thickness is kept at ~5 nm.
After the preparation of the entire stack, the samples are annealed at 200 0 C for 5 hours (pressure ~10 -6 torr) to ensure better adhesion between successive layers.

div.head: Sample characterization:
div.p: All the measurements are taken in a closed-cycle optical cryostat (Montana Instruments) at 5 K using a × 50 long-working-distance objective having a numerical aperture of 0.5. To measure the exciton DOLP �= (𝐼𝐼
div.formula: 𝐻𝐻/𝐻𝐻 -𝐼𝐼 H/V ) �𝐼𝐼 𝐻𝐻/𝐻𝐻 + 𝐼𝐼 H/V � ⁄ �,
div.p: we place the analyzer in the parallel �𝐼𝐼 𝐻𝐻/𝐻𝐻 � and perpendicular �𝐼𝐼 H/V � direction in the collection path relative to the excitation polarization direction. For the DOCP measurements, a quarterwave plate is inserted just before the objective lens, and aligned at 45 0 with respect to the incoming linearly polarized light. The time-resolved photoluminescence measurement is carried out using a 531 nm laser controlled by the PDL-800 D driver (laser pulse width is 48 ps). We use a single photon counting detector from Micro Photon Devices and the time correlated measurements are taken using the PicoHarp 300 TCSPC system (PicoQuant). We use a combination of two bandpass filters to get the time resolved counts of the 𝐴𝐴 1𝑠𝑠 exciton at The time evolution of the density operator 𝜌𝜌 for the mixed state in the presence of exchange field and momentum scattering �W 𝐐𝐐𝐐𝐐 ′ � 𝑖𝑖s given by MSS as 1 :
div.formula: 𝑑𝑑𝜌𝜌(𝐐𝐐, 𝑡𝑡) 𝑑𝑑𝑡𝑡 = 𝑖𝑖 ћ [𝜌𝜌(𝐐𝐐, 𝑡𝑡), 𝐻𝐻] + � W 𝐐𝐐𝐐𝐐 ′ [𝜌𝜌(𝐐𝐐 ′ , 𝑡𝑡) -𝜌𝜌(𝐐𝐐, 𝑡𝑡)] 𝐐𝐐 ′ - 𝜌𝜌(𝐐𝐐, 𝑡𝑡) 𝜏𝜏 + 𝐺𝐺 (1)
div.p: Here 𝜏𝜏 represents the net exciton lifetime within the light cone, and the matrix 𝐺𝐺 accounts for the exciton generation rate. 𝐻𝐻 is the total pseudospin Hamiltonian which can be decomposed into 𝐻𝐻 = 𝐻𝐻 0 + 𝐻𝐻 1 (𝐐𝐐), where 𝐻𝐻 0 is the diagonal matrix that accounts for the applied magnetic field in the out-of-plane direction:
div.formula: 𝐻𝐻 0 = � 𝐸𝐸 + 0 0 𝐸𝐸 - �
div.p: and 𝐻𝐻 1 is the matrix containing the off-diagonal elements that is responsible for the exchange driven exciton pseudospin flip:
div.formula: 𝐻𝐻 1 (𝐐𝐐) = ћΩ ∥ (𝐐𝐐) 2 � 0 𝑒𝑒 -𝑖𝑖2𝜙𝜙 𝑒𝑒 𝑖𝑖2𝜙𝜙 0 �
div.p: where 𝜙𝜙 is the angle between 𝐐𝐐 and the 𝑥𝑥 axis.
𝐻𝐻 0 can be expanded as
div.formula: 𝐻𝐻 0 = 1 2 𝑇𝑇𝑇𝑇(𝐻𝐻 0 )I + ћΩ 0 2 𝜎𝜎 𝑧𝑧
div.p: where Ω 0 = (𝐸𝐸 + -𝐸𝐸 -) ћ, ⁄ and 𝐻𝐻 1 (𝐐𝐐) can be written as
div.formula: 𝐻𝐻 1 (𝐐𝐐) = ћ Ω ∥ (𝐐𝐐) 2 �cos(2𝜙𝜙) 𝜎𝜎 𝑥𝑥 + sin(2𝜙𝜙) 𝜎𝜎 𝑦𝑦 �.
div.p: Thus, we obtain
div.formula: 𝐻𝐻 = 1 2 𝑇𝑇𝑇𝑇(𝐻𝐻 0 )I + ћ 2 𝛀𝛀. 𝝈𝝈
div.p: where 𝛀𝛀 = [Ω ∥ cos 𝜙𝜙 , Ω ∥ sin 𝜙𝜙, Ω 0 ]. Similarly, decomposing the density matrix in terms of its trace 𝑁𝑁 and the traceless part 𝐒𝐒. 𝛔𝛔, where 𝛔𝛔 denotes the Pauli matrices, the commutator [𝜌𝜌(𝐐𝐐, 𝑡𝑡), 𝐻𝐻 ] turns out to be:
div.formula: [𝜌𝜌(𝐐𝐐, 𝑡𝑡), 𝐻𝐻] = ћ 2 [𝐒𝐒(𝐐𝐐, 𝑡𝑡). 𝛔𝛔, 𝛀𝛀. 𝝈𝝈](2)
div.p: Combining equations ( 1) and ( 2
div.formula: |𝜓𝜓⟩ = cos 𝜃𝜃 2 |𝜎𝜎 𝑧𝑧+ ⟩ + sin 𝜃𝜃 2 𝑒𝑒 𝑖𝑖𝜙𝜙 |𝜎𝜎 𝑧𝑧-⟩
div.p: Thus, the degree of circular polarization (DOCP) of this single exciton qubit state can be obtained as:
div.formula: DOCP = �cos 2 𝜃𝜃 2 -sin 2 𝜃𝜃 2 � �cos 2 𝜃𝜃 2 + sin 2 𝜃𝜃 2 � = cos 𝜃𝜃 = 𝑆𝑆 𝑧𝑧
div.p: Similarly, in the basis states of the |𝜎𝜎 𝑥𝑥+ ⟩, |𝜎𝜎 𝑥𝑥-⟩ vectors that represent the two orthogonal linear polarization directions [vertical (V) and horizontal (H)], the state can also be represented as
div.formula: |𝜓𝜓⟩ = 1 √2 �cos 𝜃𝜃 2 + sin 𝜃𝜃 2 𝑒𝑒 𝑖𝑖𝜙𝜙 � |𝜎𝜎 𝑥𝑥+ ⟩ + 1 √2 �cos 𝜃𝜃 2 -sin 𝜃𝜃 2 𝑒𝑒 𝑖𝑖𝜙𝜙 � |𝜎𝜎 𝑥𝑥-⟩
div.p: in which case, the degree of linear polarization (DOLP) can be calculated as:
div.formula: DOLP = ��cos 𝜃𝜃 2 + sin 𝜃𝜃 2 𝑒𝑒 𝑖𝑖𝜙𝜙 � 2 -�cos 𝜃𝜃 2 -sin 𝜃𝜃 2 𝑒𝑒 𝑖𝑖𝜙𝜙 � 2 � ��cos 𝜃𝜃 2 + sin 𝜃𝜃 2 𝑒𝑒 𝑖𝑖𝜙𝜙 � 2 + �cos 𝜃𝜃 2 -sin 𝜃𝜃 2 𝑒𝑒 𝑖𝑖𝜙𝜙 � 2 � = sin 𝜃𝜃 cos 𝜙𝜙 = 𝑆𝑆 𝑥𝑥
div.p: The overall steady state DOLP is then obtained as 〈𝑆𝑆 𝑥𝑥 〉 averaged over the 𝐐𝐐 space.

div.head: Supplementary Note 3: DOLP is greater than DOCP in a 2D system
div.p: To show that the exciton DOLP is generally higher than its DOCP value in 2D semiconductors, we take a special case of no scattering scenario (W 𝐐𝐐𝐐𝐐 ′ → 0). The steady-state form of the MSS equation is then given by:
div.formula: 𝐆𝐆 = 1 𝜏𝜏 𝐒𝐒(𝐐𝐐) -𝛀𝛀(𝐐𝐐) × 𝐒𝐒(𝐐𝐐) 𝛀𝛀 = [Ω ∥ cos 𝜙𝜙 , Ω ∥ sin 𝜙𝜙, 0]
div.p: in the absence of an external magnetic field, and 𝜙𝜙 is the azimuthal angle between 𝐐𝐐 and 𝑥𝑥 axis.
The steady state value of 𝑆𝑆 𝑧𝑧 can be obtained by putting 𝐺𝐺 𝑥𝑥 = 0, 𝐺𝐺 𝑦𝑦 = 0 𝑎𝑎𝑎𝑎𝑑𝑑 𝐺𝐺 𝑧𝑧 = 𝐺𝐺 in the above equation as
div.formula: 𝑆𝑆 𝑧𝑧 (𝐐𝐐) = 𝐺𝐺𝜏𝜏 (1 + 𝜏𝜏 2 Ω ∥ 2 (𝐐𝐐))
div.p: Similarly, the steady state value of 𝑆𝑆 𝑥𝑥 can be obtained by putting 𝐺𝐺 𝑥𝑥 = 𝐺𝐺, 𝐺𝐺 𝑦𝑦 = 0 𝑎𝑎𝑎𝑎𝑑𝑑 𝐺𝐺 𝑧𝑧 = 0:
div.formula: 𝑆𝑆 𝑥𝑥 (𝐐𝐐) = 𝐺𝐺𝜏𝜏 (1 + 𝜏𝜏 2 Ω ∥ 2 (𝐐𝐐)cos 2 2𝜙𝜙) (1 + 𝜏𝜏 2 Ω ∥ 2 (𝐐𝐐))
div.p: After the initial generation along the 0 0 , 180 0 axis, the degree of linear polarization 𝑆𝑆 𝑥𝑥 (𝐐𝐐) at those 𝐐𝐐 values in the limiting condition of W 𝐐𝐐𝐐𝐐 ′ → 0 (𝜙𝜙 → 0 0 , 180 0 ) is 𝐺𝐺𝜏𝜏. Hence, we can see that, in the case of no scattering, the pseudospin 𝑆𝑆 𝑥𝑥 is nearly independent of Ω ∥, whereas 𝑆𝑆 𝑧𝑧 suffers due to Ω ∥ . A physical explanation for this is that the linear polarization degrades because of one of the in-plane magnetic field components, but the out-of-plane circular polarization experiences the effect of both the in-plane components of the exchange magnetic field 1,2 . As a result, the DOLP is generally higher than the DOCP in these 2D exciton. for the conduction and valence band, respectively.

div.head: Supplementary
div.p: In the basis of the combined CB (|𝑐𝑐, 𝐤𝐤 + 𝐐𝐐⟩) and VB (|𝑣𝑣, 𝐤𝐤⟩) pair states, denoted together as |𝑣𝑣𝑐𝑐𝐤𝐤𝐐𝐐⟩, where 𝐐𝐐 (= 𝐤𝐤 𝐞𝐞 + 𝐤𝐤 𝐡𝐡 ) is the exciton center-of-mass momentum, the exciton band structure is calculated by solving the following Bethe-Salpeter equation 4 :
div.formula: ⟨𝑣𝑣𝑐𝑐𝐤𝐤𝐐𝐐|𝐻𝐻|𝑣𝑣𝑐𝑐𝐤𝐤 ′ 𝐐𝐐⟩ = 𝛿𝛿 𝑘𝑘𝑘𝑘 ′ �𝐸𝐸 𝑐𝑐,𝐤𝐤+𝐐𝐐 -𝐸𝐸 𝑣𝑣,𝐤𝐤 � -(𝐷𝐷 -𝑋𝑋)(𝐤𝐤, 𝐤𝐤 ′ , 𝐐𝐐)
div.p: 𝐷𝐷, 𝑋𝑋 are the direct and the exchange interaction terms. We neglect the contribution of exchange interaction at small 𝐐𝐐 within the light cone in the exciton band structure calculation. The direct coulombic interaction is given by:
div.formula: 𝐷𝐷 = 1 𝐴𝐴 𝑉𝑉 𝑘𝑘-𝑘𝑘 ′ ⟨𝑐𝑐, 𝐤𝐤 + 𝐐𝐐|𝑐𝑐, 𝐤𝐤 ′ + 𝐐𝐐⟩ ⟨𝑣𝑣, 𝐤𝐤 ′ |𝑣𝑣, 𝐤𝐤⟩
div.p: Here 𝐴𝐴 is the area of the two-dimensional crystal and 𝑉𝑉 𝑘𝑘-𝑘𝑘 ′ is the interaction potential given by 5 :
div.formula: 𝑉𝑉 𝑞𝑞 = 2𝜋𝜋𝑞𝑞 0 2 𝑒𝑒 -𝑞𝑞𝑞𝑞 𝑞𝑞 1 𝜖𝜖(𝑞𝑞)
div.p: Here 𝑞𝑞 0 is the magnitude of the charge of an electron, 𝑞𝑞 = |𝐤𝐤 -𝐤𝐤 ′ |, and 𝜉𝜉 is a fitting parameter that reflects the extension of the wavefunction of the electron and the hole in the out-of-plane direction. The dielectric function 𝜖𝜖(𝑞𝑞) is given by
div.formula: 𝜖𝜖(𝑞𝑞) = (1 -𝑝𝑝 𝑏𝑏 𝑝𝑝 𝑡𝑡 𝑒𝑒 -2𝜂𝜂𝑞𝑞𝜂𝜂 )𝜅𝜅 (1 -𝑝𝑝 𝑡𝑡 𝑒𝑒 -𝜂𝜂𝑞𝑞𝜂𝜂 )(1 -𝑝𝑝 𝑏𝑏 𝑒𝑒 -𝜂𝜂𝑞𝑞𝜂𝜂 ) + 𝑇𝑇 0 𝑞𝑞𝑒𝑒 -𝑞𝑞𝑞𝑞

div.head: DOLP calculation
div.p: The DOLP for the three stacks is calculated using the steady-state form of the MSS equation given as:
div.formula: 𝐆𝐆 = 1 𝜏𝜏 𝐒𝐒(𝐐𝐐) -𝛀𝛀(𝐐𝐐) × 𝐒𝐒(𝐐𝐐) -� W 𝐐𝐐𝐐𝐐 ′ [𝐒𝐒(𝐐𝐐 ′ ) -𝐒𝐒(𝐐𝐐)]
𝐻𝐻 𝐐𝐐 ′ 𝐐𝐐 = � 𝑞𝑞 0 2 4𝜋𝜋𝜖𝜖 0 𝜖𝜖 𝑠𝑠 𝐴𝐴 � � 𝑒𝑒 -𝑖𝑖𝑖𝑖𝑟𝑟 cos 𝜙𝜙 𝑒𝑒 -𝑟𝑟 𝐿𝐿 𝐷𝐷 ⁄ 𝑑𝑑𝑇𝑇 𝑑𝑑𝜙𝜙
div.p: The above expression after integration over 𝑇𝑇 results in
div.formula: 𝐻𝐻 𝐐𝐐 ′ 𝐐𝐐 = � 𝑞𝑞 0 2 𝐿𝐿 𝜂𝜂 4𝜋𝜋𝜖𝜖 0 𝜖𝜖 𝑠𝑠 𝐴𝐴 � � � 1 1 + 𝛽𝛽 2 𝐿𝐿 𝜂𝜂 2 cos 2 𝜙𝜙 𝑑𝑑𝜙𝜙 -𝑖𝑖 � 𝛽𝛽𝐿𝐿 𝜂𝜂 cos 𝜙𝜙 1 + 𝛽𝛽 2 𝐿𝐿 𝜂𝜂 2 cos 2 𝜙𝜙 𝑑𝑑𝜙𝜙�
div.p: The result of the first integration is 2𝜋𝜋 �1 + 𝛽𝛽 2 𝐿𝐿 𝜂𝜂 2 ⁄ and the second one is zero. The matrix element thus becomes
div.formula: 𝐻𝐻 𝐐𝐐 ′ 𝐐𝐐 = 𝑞𝑞 0 2 2𝜖𝜖 0 𝜖𝜖 𝑠𝑠 𝐴𝐴 �𝛽𝛽 2 + 1 𝐿𝐿 𝜂𝜂 2 ⁄
div.p: We assume the limiting case of 𝐿𝐿 𝜂𝜂 → ∞ and obtain the following expression for the scattering rate W 𝐐𝐐 ′ 𝐐𝐐 as
div.formula: W 𝐐𝐐 ′ 𝐐𝐐 ∝ �𝐻𝐻 𝐐𝐐 ′ 𝐐𝐐 � 2 = � 𝑞𝑞 0 2 2𝜖𝜖 0 𝜖𝜖 𝑠𝑠 𝐴𝐴 𝛽𝛽 � 2
div.p: where 𝛽𝛽 =

figure.figDesc: K: 650 (FWHM -55 nm) and 635 nm bandpass filter (FWHM -10 nm) for the HMH and the GHMHG stack, and 650 (FWHM -55 nm) and 660 nm bandpass filter (FWHM -10 nm) for the GMG stack. The instrument response function (IRF) has an FWHM of 52 ps, and shows a decay of 23 ps. The deconvolution of the TRPL data with the IRF is carried out using the QuCoa software (PicoQuant). .(HMH) = 379 meV B.E.(GHMHG) = 162.5 meV B.E.(GMG) = 122 meV

figure.head: Figure 1 :
figure.figDesc: Figure 1: Mechanism of exciton valley decoherence and the factors affecting it. (a) Top view of a ring inside the light cone of the exciton band showing the exciton decoherence dynamics due to scattering W 𝐐𝐐𝐐𝐐 ′ within the light cone (purple dashed arrows) and subsequent precession because of inter-valley exchange induced pseudo-magnetic field (black solid arrows). (b) The direction of 𝐒𝐒 at generation (𝑡𝑡 = 0) and recombination (𝑡𝑡 = 𝜏𝜏) are shown in the Bloch sphere. (c) The calculated DOLP as a function of scattering rate with motional narrowing regime shown in shade. (d) The decoherence of 𝐒𝐒 (green arrows) with time due to scattering (dashed purple arrow) and precession around 𝛀𝛀 (black arrows) for an exciton for three different scenarios. The effect of screening versus screening + filtering on valley decoherence is compared in the three rows.

figure.head: Figure 2 :
figure.figDesc: Figure 2: Excitonic emission from different stacks along with DOLP and DOCP. (a-d) PL spectra with near-resonant 633 nm linearly polarized excitation in co-(H/H) and cross-(H/V) polarized detection configuration in the (a) hBN-MoS2-hBN (HMH) stack, (b) FLG-hBN-MoS2-hBN-FLG (GHMHG) stack, (c) MoS2-FLG-hBN (MGH) stack, and the (d) FLG-MoS2-FLG (GMG) stack at 𝑇𝑇 = 5 K. 𝐴𝐴 1𝑠𝑠 and 𝐴𝐴 1𝑠𝑠 -represent the exciton and the trion peak, respectively. (e) Bar graph comparing the experimental DOLP values along with the error bars in the four stacks. (f) PL spectra obtained from FLG-TMD-FLG stack (using 532 nm excitation) for monolayer MoS2, MoSe2, and WS2 showing the prominent 𝐴𝐴 1𝑠𝑠 and 𝐴𝐴 2𝑠𝑠 peaks. The 𝐴𝐴 2𝑠𝑠 -𝐴𝐴 1𝑠𝑠 separation is around 44 (32) meV in MoS2 (MoSe2, WS2). The peaks marked as * and ⋅ are the 2D and the G Raman peaks of the FLG. (g-h) Representative PL spectra taken with circularly polarized excitation in co-(𝜎𝜎 +/𝜎𝜎+) and cross-(𝜎𝜎 +/𝜎𝜎-) polarized detection configuration. The corresponding DOCP value of the 𝐴𝐴 1𝑠𝑠 exciton in (g) the HMH and (h) the GMG stack at 𝑇𝑇 = 5 K is shown in the inset. The peaks indicated by the dashed lines in (d) and (h) represent the prominent Raman peaks in the GMG stack due to dual resonance.

figure.head: Figure 3 :
figure.figDesc: Figure 3: Evidence of graphene induced screening of electron-hole exchange interaction. (a-c) Top panel: Schematic representation of the screening effect in the different stacks. Bottom panel: PL spectra taken with 532 nm excitation highlighting the different degree of dielectric screening in our samples. The 𝐴𝐴 2𝑠𝑠 -𝐴𝐴 1𝑠𝑠 separation for the HMH stack (144.5 meV), GHMHG stack (63.5 meV), and the GMG stack (43.3 meV) is indicated by the dashed arrows. * in (c) is the 2D Raman peak of FLG. (d) Eigen energies (open circle) and the corresponding binding energies of the 𝐴𝐴 1𝑠𝑠 exciton obtained from the Bethe-Salpeter equation. The solid symbols

figure.head: Figure 4 : 2 𝑒𝑒 𝑖𝑖𝜙𝜙 sin 𝜃𝜃 2 �
figure.figDesc: Figure 4: Comparison of the experimental data with the steady-state solution of the MSS equation. Simulation results comparing the exciton DOLP (〈𝑆𝑆 𝑥𝑥 〉) as a function of the scaling factor (𝑤𝑤) of the scattering rate (bottom axis) for the HMH stack (solid black trace) and the GMG stack (dashed black trace). The downward trend in the left-hand side (unshaded region) is the low-scattering regime, and the upward trend in the right-hand side (shaded region) is the motional narrowing regime. Overlapped on the simulation results is the experimentally obtained DOLP variation with the Lorentzian linewidth (Γ ℎ𝑜𝑜𝑜𝑜 ) of the corresponding copolarized PL spectrum (top axis) for the HMH stack (solid spheres) and the GMG stack (open circles). The upward trend of the experimental data suggests that both the stacks are operating in the motional narrowing regime.

figure.head: Note 4: Simulation Details 4 . 1 . 1 √2��𝑑𝑑 𝑥𝑥 2 -
figure.figDesc: Electronic and Excitonic band structure calculation We use 2 × 2 k.p Hamiltonian to get the band dispersion of the lowest energy conduction band (CB) and highest energy valence band (VB) in the 𝐊𝐊, 𝐊𝐊 ′ valley in monolayer TMDs. The Hamiltonian in the atomic orbital basis states of |𝑑𝑑 𝑧𝑧 2 ⟩ and 𝑦𝑦 2 � + 𝑖𝑖𝜏𝜏�𝑑𝑑 𝑥𝑥𝑦𝑦 ��, where 𝜏𝜏 (= ±1) represents the valley index, is given by 3 : � 𝐸𝐸 𝑔𝑔 𝑎𝑎𝑡𝑡(𝜏𝜏𝑘𝑘 𝑥𝑥 -𝑖𝑖𝑘𝑘 𝑦𝑦 ) 𝑎𝑎𝑡𝑡(𝜏𝜏𝑘𝑘 𝑥𝑥 + 𝑖𝑖𝑘𝑘 𝑦𝑦 ) 0 � 𝑘𝑘 𝑥𝑥 , 𝑘𝑘 𝑦𝑦 are the wave vectors in the reciprocal space. The value of the lattice constant 𝑎𝑎, the hopping amplitude 𝑡𝑡, and the quasi-particle band gap 𝐸𝐸 𝑔𝑔 for calculating monolayer MoS2 band structure are taken as 3.193 Å, 1.10 eV and 2.4 eV, respectively. The eigen energies (eigen functions) obtained after solving the above Hamiltonian is denoted as 𝐸𝐸 𝑐𝑐,𝐤𝐤 (|𝑐𝑐, 𝐤𝐤⟩), 𝐸𝐸 𝑣𝑣,𝐤𝐤 (|𝑣𝑣, 𝐤𝐤⟩)

figure.head: For the case ofSupplementary Figure 2 :
figure.figDesc: identical dielectric environment on top and bottom, 𝑝𝑝 𝑏𝑏 = 𝑝𝑝 𝑡𝑡 = 𝑃𝑃 = (𝜖𝜖 𝐸𝐸𝐸𝐸𝐸𝐸 -𝜅𝜅) (𝜖𝜖 𝐸𝐸𝐸𝐸𝐸𝐸 + 𝜅𝜅) ⁄ . 𝐷𝐷 is the thickness of the monolayer sheet, 𝜂𝜂 = �𝜖𝜖 ∥ 𝜖𝜖 ⊥ ⁄ and 𝜅𝜅 = � 𝜖𝜖 ∥ 𝜖𝜖 ⊥ . 𝜖𝜖 ∥ (𝜖𝜖 ⊥ ) and 𝜖𝜖 𝐸𝐸𝐸𝐸𝐸𝐸 is the effective in-plane (out-of-plane) and environmental dielectric constant of the monolayer TMD. We use the high frequency dielectric constant value of 𝜖𝜖 ∥ = 5.01 and 𝜖𝜖 ⊥ = 6.07 in our calculations. The fitting parameters for the HMH, GHMHG and the GMG stacks are summarized below: With these fitting parameters, we ensure that the calculated 𝐴𝐴 2𝑠𝑠 -𝐴𝐴 1𝑠𝑠 energy separation matches with the experimentally obtained value. The exciton energy spectrum for the first few states showing the 𝐴𝐴 1𝑠𝑠 exciton binding energy change in the three stacks is plotted in the main text (Figure 3d), and the full spectrum is shown below. The calculated exciton eigen energy spectrum (solid lines) for the HMH (in red), GHMHG (in blue), and the GMG (in green) stack obtained after solving the Bethe-Salpeter equation. The dashed lines represent the respective continuum energy levels. The extracted 𝐴𝐴 1𝑠𝑠 exciton binding energy is 379 meV in the HMH stack. The binding energy reduces to 162.5 and 122 meV in the GHMHG and the GMG stack, respectively due to dielectric screening.

figure.head: 𝜏𝜏 2 4𝜋𝜋𝜖𝜖 0
figure.figDesc: is the net exciton lifetime, 𝐆𝐆 is the exciton generation rate, W 𝐐𝐐𝐐𝐐 ′ is the exciton scattering rate within the light cone, and Ω ∥ (𝐐𝐐) (= 2|𝐽𝐽 𝐐𝐐 𝐿𝐿𝐿𝐿 | ћ ⁄ ) is the precession frequency magnitude due to intervalley exchange induced magnetic field, where 𝐽𝐽 𝐐𝐐 𝐿𝐿𝐿𝐿 is the long-range component of exchange, and is given by 6 : 𝐐𝐐) |𝐐𝐐| 2 𝑒𝑒 -2𝑖𝑖𝜙𝜙 𝜙𝜙 is the azimuthal angle in the 𝐐𝐐-space. The screening modifies the exchange interaction through the change in the electron-hole wavefunction overlap |∑ 𝜓𝜓(𝐤𝐤) 𝐤𝐤 | 2 𝐴𝐴 ⁄ , bandgap 𝐸𝐸 𝑔𝑔 and the Fourier potential 𝑉𝑉(𝐐𝐐). In Figure 3e of the main text, the magnitude of the exchange interaction potential is plotted as a function of |𝐐𝐐| within the light cone. During the calculation, we fit the parameters such that the 𝐴𝐴 2𝑠𝑠 -𝐴𝐴 1𝑠𝑠 separation remains the same as that obtained experimentally. 𝜏𝜏 is the exciton lifetime. For W 𝐐𝐐𝐐𝐐 ′ , we take the exciton-impurity scattering within the light cone as the only scattering mechanism at 5 K, neglecting exciton-phonon scattering. For simplicity, to get the exciton-impurity scattering matrix element, we use a similar form of the perturbing potential expression given for electron-ionized impurity scattering. We use the generic screened coulomb potential form as 7 𝑞𝑞 0 𝜖𝜖 𝑠𝑠 𝑇𝑇 𝑒𝑒 -𝑟𝑟 𝐿𝐿 𝐷𝐷 ⁄ 𝜖𝜖 0 is the dielectric permittivity of vacuum and 𝐿𝐿 𝜂𝜂 is the Debye length. The matrix element for the above perturbing potential for an exciton scattering from 𝐐𝐐 to 𝐐𝐐 ′ state is given by 𝐻𝐻 𝐐𝐐 ′ 𝐐𝐐 = 𝑠𝑠 𝐴𝐴 � � 𝑒𝑒 𝑖𝑖(𝐐𝐐-𝐐𝐐 ′ ).𝐫𝐫 𝑒𝑒 -𝑟𝑟 𝐿𝐿 𝐷𝐷 ⁄ 𝑑𝑑𝑇𝑇 𝑑𝑑𝜙𝜙 Putting 𝐐𝐐 ′ -𝐐𝐐 = 𝛃𝛃 (note that, |𝐐𝐐 ′ | = |𝐐𝐐| since Coulomb scattering is elastic in nature), we obtain

figure.head: 2𝑄𝑄 ћ sin 𝛼𝛼 2 ⁄Supplementary Figure 3 :
figure.figDesc: , 𝛼𝛼 is the angle between the initial (𝐐𝐐) and the final (𝐐𝐐 ′ ) exciton state.The final form of the steady state MSS equation after plugging in the above form of scattering rate W 𝐐𝐐 ′ 𝐐𝐐 as given in the main text is given as:𝐆𝐆 = 1 𝜏𝜏 𝐒𝐒(𝐐𝐐) -𝛀𝛀(𝐐𝐐) × 𝐒𝐒(𝐐𝐐) -� 𝑤𝑤 𝑄𝑄 2 sin 2 𝛼𝛼 2 [𝐒𝐒(𝐐𝐐 ′ ) -𝐒𝐒(𝐐𝐐)] 𝐐𝐐 ′where 𝑤𝑤 is used as a fitting parameter. Finally, the DOLP (= 〈𝑆𝑆 𝑥𝑥 〉) is calculated by averaging the calculated pseudospin at the 𝐐𝐐-values within the light cone and is plotted in Figure4in the main text for the HMH and the GMG sample. The co-(H/H, in red) and cross-(H/V, in blue) linearly polarized PL spectra obtained at different spots on the HMH stack in the increasing order of DOLP. The range of the DOLP values obtained lies between 26 -64 %.

figure.head: Supplementary Figure 4 :Supplementary Figure 5 :
figure.figDesc: The co-(H/H, in red) and cross-(H/V, in blue) linearly polarized PL spectra obtained at different spots on the GHMHG stack. The DOLP values range within 21 -46 % in this stack. The co-(H/H, in red) and cross-(H/V, in blue) linearly polarized PL spectra obtained at different spots on the MGH stack. The DOLP values range within 69 -

figure.head: Supplementary Note 2: Derivation that DOLP is given by 〈𝑺𝑺 𝒙𝒙

row.cell: I + 𝐒𝐒(𝐐𝐐 ′ , 𝑡𝑡). 𝛔𝛔 -𝐒𝐒(𝐐𝐐, 𝑡𝑡). 𝛔𝛔�
On equating the traceless matrices on both the sides of the above equation, we get:

note.p: + � W 𝐐𝐐𝐐𝐐 ′ ( 𝐒𝐒(𝐐𝐐 ′ , 𝑡𝑡). 𝛔𝛔 -𝐒𝐒(𝐐𝐐, 𝑡𝑡). 𝛔𝛔) 𝐐𝐐 ′ -1 𝜏𝜏 (𝐒𝐒(𝐐𝐐, 𝑡𝑡). 𝛔𝛔) + 𝐆𝐆. 𝛔𝛔 where 𝐆𝐆 = [𝐺𝐺 𝑥𝑥 𝐺𝐺 𝑦𝑦 𝐺𝐺 𝑧𝑧 ] is the generation rate vector.By expanding the commutation bracket and using the vector identity (𝐒𝐒. 𝛔𝛔)(𝛀𝛀. 𝝈𝝈) = (𝐒𝐒. 𝛀𝛀)I + 𝑖𝑖(𝐒𝐒 × 𝛀𝛀). 𝝈𝝈, we get the final form of the MSS equation as:𝑑𝑑𝐒𝐒(𝐐𝐐) 𝑑𝑑𝑡𝑡 = 𝛀𝛀(𝐐𝐐) × 𝐒𝐒(𝐐𝐐) + � W 𝐐𝐐𝐐𝐐 ′ [𝐒𝐒(𝐐𝐐 ′ ) -𝐒𝐒(𝐐𝐐)] 〉The state |𝜓𝜓⟩ in the basis states of two-orthogonal circular polarizations |𝜎𝜎 𝑧𝑧+ ⟩, |𝜎𝜎 𝑧𝑧-⟩ is expressed as:

div.head: Acknowledgement
div.p: This work was supported in part by a Core Research Grant from the Science and Engineering Research Board (SERB) under Department of Science and Technology (DST), a grant from Indian Space Research Organization (ISRO), a grant from MHRD under STARS, and a grant from MHRD, MeitY and DST Nano Mission through NNetRA. K.W. and T.T. acknowledge support from the JSPS KAKENHI (Grant Numbers 19H05790 and 20H00354).

div.head: Supplementary Information:
div.p: Observation of ~100% valley-coherent excitons in monolayer MoS 2 through giant enhancement of valley coherence time Garima Gupta 1 , Kenji Watanabe 2 , Takashi Taniguchi 3 , and Kausik Majumdar 1, * 1 Department of Electrical Communication Engineering, Indian Institute of Science, Bangalore 560012, India 2 Research Center for Functional Materials, National Institute for Materials Science, 1-1 Namiki, Tsukuba 305-044, Japan

analytic.title: Exciton binding energy and nonhydrogenic Rydberg series in monolayer WS2
Observation of excitonic Rydberg states in monolayer MoS2 and WS2 by photoluminescence excitation spectroscopy
Probing excitonic dark states in single-layer tungsten disulphide
Control of valley polarization in monolayer MoS2 by optical helicity
Valley polarization in MoS 2 monolayers by optical pumping
Valley-selective circular dichroism of monolayer molybdenum disulphide
Carrier and polarization dynamics in monolayer MoS 2
Optical generation of excitonic valley coherence in monolayer WSe 2
